acceptance.criterion: The snapshot handles authenticated and unauthenticated installs honestly, records unavailable capabilities with reasons, and never contains credentials or raw OAuth output.
The capability ledger proves the supported read-only, session, goal, streaming, schema, and ACP surfaces and explicitly identifies `--new-session` as unsupported.
A model-free isolated `/goal status` probe verifies goal-command resolution independently from provider authentication and model inference.
Batch 0 is additive: it changes no product launch argv or non-Grok adapter behavior.
Read-only, create, resume, and trusted full-run argv contain only installed-binary-supported flags, and new sessions use a caller-generated UUID through `--session-id`.
OAuth launches retain private `HOME` and `GROK_HOME` plus the validated narrow `GROK_AUTH_PATH`; ambient config, SSH state, and git identity are not inherited.
XAI API-key behavior and every non-Grok adapter's emitted argv remain unchanged.
A reduced-capability or unsupported Grok install fails or falls back with a concrete reason instead of entering an interactive login or invalid-flag loop.
Grok provider qualification is independent from goal support, and an unavailable goal capability selects the documented one-packet fallback without disabling an otherwise valid provider.
An isolated authenticated throwaway-repository canary proves that a headless `/goal` launch accepts the packet-backed objective, completes, and returns an exact recoverable session identity.
The streaming follow view exposes sanitized progress, usage, terminal state, and typed errors; it tolerates unknown event types and never requires timed driver narration.
Worker branch, commit, crash recovery, acceptance, protected-ref, PR, and merge authority remain identical to the provider-neutral full-run contract.
Model selection uses the authenticated live catalog and parsed default; no unavailable model, including `auto` or `grok-code-fast-1`, is selected unless the catalog returns it.
A user can install, authenticate, capability-check, launch, follow, and recover the optional open-source Grok worker from one concise documentation path.
SKILL, AGENTS, Claude/Codex references, README, guide, examples, changelog, and learnings agree on native-first routing, goal enhancement, one-packet fallback, and authority boundaries.
Installed Codex and Claude Code bundle checks pass, and native worker routing and invocation behavior remain unchanged.

continuation_guard.next_required_action: Launch the High Prewalk turn, then resume its exact Codex session at Medium for the complete run.
continuation_guard.reason: The user's single kickoff covers staging, internal Prewalk handoff, implementation, and final reviewed-PR readiness.

master_acceptance.criterion: Against installed open-source Grok Build, the optional read-only and trusted full-run lanes emit no invalid flags and use capability-proven goal plus streaming behavior or the documented compatible fallback.
Authentication isolation, session create/resume identity, model selection, crash recovery, and worker authority match the executable and provider-neutral contracts exactly.
Native Codex and Claude Code remain the default and behaviorally unchanged, while all user-facing and installed Elves documentation accurately describes the optional open-source Grok path.
Focused proof, one cumulative final review, targeted revisions, and terminal repository checks leave a reviewed PR ready for the user without merging or moving a version tag.
